intro:
The imprecatory psalms are portions of the Psalter calling down divine judgment, destruction, or calamity upon enemies: 'Let death take my enemies by surprise; let them go down alive to the realm of the dead' (Ps. 55:15); 'May his days be few; may another take his place of leadership' (Ps. 109:8, cited in Acts 1:20); 'Happy is the one who seizes your infants and dashes them against the rocks' (Ps. 137:9). Psalms 35, 58, 69, 79, 83, and 109 contain the most extensive imprecations. These passages have been among the most challenging for Christian readers, appearing to contradict Jesus's command to love enemies (Matt. 5:44) and Paul's prohibition of personal vengeance (Rom. 12:19).

Several interpretive approaches have been proposed. The supersessionist view holds that these psalms represent a lower, pre-Christian moral level superseded by the NT — a position criticized for creating a truncated canon. The typological view reads them as the voice of Christ against his ultimate enemies rather than expressions of personal vengeance. The theological view, supported by recent scholarship, notes that the imprecatory psalms are petitions for divine justice rather than acts of personal revenge — they cry out for God to vindicate his covenant, defend the oppressed, and punish evil, which is the role of divine justice and not human vengeance (cf. Rev. 6:10: 'How long, Sovereign Lord, until you judge the inhabitants of the earth and avenge our blood?'). When directed toward God as petition rather than enacted as personal violence, these prayers express righteous indignation against evil and confidence in God as the just judge of history — emotions appropriate to genuine faith under severe persecution.
